questions.q: Which equation is NOT part of the definition of a category?
choices: $g\circ f = f\circ g$ whenever both composites are defined
questions.explain: Composition in a category is associative and unital, but not commutative. In $\mathsf{Set}$ already, $g\circ f\ne f\circ g$ in general.

questions.q: In a category $\mathcal{C}$, an isomorphism $f\colon A\to B$ is a morphism such that:
choices: $f$ is a bijection on underlying sets
there exists $g\colon B\to A$ with $g\circ f=\mathrm{id}_A$ and $f\circ g=\mathrm{id}_B$
questions.explain: Isomorphism is a categorical notion: a morphism with a two-sided inverse. Only in $\mathsf{Set}$ does this specialize to bijection.

questions.q: How many morphisms does the category $\mathbf{2}=(\bullet\xrightarrow{f}\bullet)$ with two objects and one non-identity arrow have in total (including identities)?
questions.explain: Two identities $\mathrm{id}_0,\mathrm{id}_1$ plus the single arrow $f$ gives $3$ morphisms.

hard.q: A morphism $f\colon A\to B$ in a category is a monomorphism if and only if:
choices: for all $g,h\colon C\to A$, $f\circ g = f\circ h$ implies $g=h$
for all $g,h\colon B\to C$, $g\circ f = h\circ f$ implies $g=h$
hard.explain: A monomorphism is left-cancellable: $f\circ g=f\circ h\Rightarrow g=h$. This is the categorical generalisation of injectivity. Having a left inverse implies mono, but not conversely — e.g. inclusions in $\mathsf{Top}$ can be mono without being split.

hard.q: Consider the category $\mathcal{B}G$ associated to a group $G$ (one object $*$, morphisms = elements of $G$, composition = multiplication). A functor $F\colon\mathcal{B}G\to\mathsf{Set}$ is the same as:
hard.explain: $F$ must send $*$ to some set $S=F(*)$ and each $g\in G$ to a function $S\to S$, preserving composition and identity. This is precisely the data of a left $G$-set (group action on $S$).

hard.q: The category of a poset $(P,\le)$ has a morphism $x\to y$ iff $x\le y$. In the poset $\{0<1<2\}$, how many morphisms (including identities) are there in total?
hard.explain: Identities: $0\to 0$, $1\to 1$, $2\to 2$ (3 morphisms). Non-identity morphisms: $0\to 1$, $1\to 2$, $0\to 2$ (3 morphisms). Total: $6$.

questions.q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ must satisfy which property on composition?
choices: $F$ need not respect composition — only objects
questions.explain: Covariant functors preserve the order of composition: $F(g\circ f)=F(g)\circ F(f)$. Contravariant functors reverse it — those are functors out of $\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}$.

questions.q: A natural transformation $\eta\colon F\Rightarrow G$ between $F,G\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ consists of components $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$ satisfying, for every $f\colon A\to B$ in $\mathcal{C}$:
questions.explain: The naturality square: $G(f)\circ\eta_A=\eta_B\circ F(f)$. The transformation commutes with the action of $f$ on both sides.

questions.q: How many functors are there from the terminal category $\mathbf{1}$ (one object, only identity) to a category $\mathcal{C}$ with exactly $5$ objects?
questions.explain: A functor $\mathbf{1}\to\mathcal{C}$ just picks an object of $\mathcal{C}$, so the count is $|\mathrm{Ob}(\mathcal{C})|=5$.

hard.q: The evaluation map $\mathrm{ev}_V\colon V\to V^{**}$, $v\mapsto(\varphi\mapsto\varphi(v))$, is a natural transformation $\mathrm{id}_{\mathsf{Vect}_k}\Rightarrow(-)^{**}$. Why is there NO natural isomorphism $\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow(-)^*$ (single dual) on all of $\mathsf{Vect}_k$?
choices: $(-)^*$ is a covariant functor, so no natural transformation can exist
$(-)^*$ is contravariant, so its domain and codomain categories differ from those of $\mathrm{id}$
Any isomorphism $V\cong V^*$ requires a choice of basis and cannot be made natural
hard.hint: What direction does $(-)^*$ send a linear map $f\colon V\to W$?
hard.explain: $(-)^*=\operatorname{Hom}(-,k)$ is contravariant: it reverses the direction of linear maps. A natural transformation requires components in the same ambient functor category, which forces both functors to have the same variance. Since $\mathrm{id}$ is covariant and $(-)^*$ is contravariant, no natural transformation — let alone isomorphism — between them can be written down.

hard.q: Arrange the steps to verify that a family $\eta=\{\eta_A\}$ is a natural transformation $F\Rightarrow G$. Which ordering is logically correct?
choices: Fix $f\colon A\to B$; (ii) form $G(f)\circ\eta_A$; (iii) form $\eta_B\circ F(f)$; (iv) show equality
Fix $A$; (ii) define $\eta_A\colon F(A)\to G(A)$; (iii) fix $f\colon A\to B$; (iv) check $G(f)\circ\eta_A=\eta_B\circ F(f)$
Show each $\eta_A$ is an isomorphism; (ii) conclude naturality
Check $\eta$ commutes with identities; (ii) conclude it commutes with all morphisms by induction
hard.explain: One must first define all components $\eta_A$, then verify the naturality square for each morphism $f$. Step (i) fixes an arbitrary morphism; steps (ii–iii) spell out both paths around the square; step (iv) proves them equal using the definitions of $F$, $G$, and $\eta$. Isomorphism is not required for a natural transformation.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the discrete category on $3$ objects (no non-identity morphisms). How many natural transformations are there from the identity functor $\mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{C}}$ to itself?
hard.explain: A natural transformation $\eta\colon\mathrm{id}\Rightarrow\mathrm{id}$ needs, for each object $A$, a morphism $\eta_A\colon A\to A$. In a discrete category the only morphism $A\to A$ is $\mathrm{id}_A$. So there is exactly $1$ natural transformation (all components forced to be identities).

questions.q: The Yoneda lemma states that for a locally small category $\mathcal{C}$, a functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$, and $X\in\mathcal{C}$, the natural transformations $h_X\Rightarrow F$ are in bijection with:
questions.explain: Yoneda: $\mathrm{Nat}(h_X,F)\cong F(X)$ naturally in $X$ and $F$. Evaluating at $\mathrm{id}_X$ recovers the element, and any $x\in F(X)$ determines the transformation $f\mapsto F(f)(x)$.

questions.q: If $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{D}$ is left adjoint to $G\colon\mathcal{D}\to\mathcal{C}$, which is true?
questions.explain: Left adjoints preserve colimits (LAPC); right adjoints preserve limits (RAPL). The adjunction $\operatorname{Hom}(F(-),-)\cong\operatorname{Hom}(-,G(-))$ together with Yoneda makes this immediate.

questions.q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the product (= categorical limit of the discrete two-object diagram) of $\{a,b,c\}$ and $\{0,1\}$ has how many elements?
questions.explain: The product in $\mathsf{Set}$ is the Cartesian product, so $|A\times B|=|A|\cdot|B|=3\cdot 2=6$.

hard.q: The Yoneda embedding $h_{(-)}\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathsf{Fun}(\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}},\mathsf{Set})$ is fully faithful. What does full faithfulness imply about objects of $\mathcal{C}$?
choices: $\mathcal{C}$ embeds as a full subcategory; in particular $X\cong Y$ in $\mathcal{C}$ iff $h_X\cong h_Y$ as functors
The embedding is an equivalence of categories
hard.explain: Full faithfulness gives $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(X,Y)\cong\operatorname{Nat}(h_X,h_Y)$ (by Yoneda with $F=h_Y$). In particular $X\cong Y\Leftrightarrow h_X\cong h_Y$: objects are determined up to isomorphism by their functor of points. Not every presheaf need be representable (that would make the embedding an equivalence, which is usually false).

hard.q: For the free–forgetful adjunction $F\dashv U\colon\mathsf{Grp}\to\mathsf{Set}$, the unit $\eta_X\colon X\to UF(X)$ is the insertion-of-generators map. What does the counit $\varepsilon_G\colon FU(G)\to G$ do?
choices: It sends every element of $G$ to the identity
It is the unique group homomorphism extending the identity $\mathrm{id}_G\colon G\to G$ along the insertion $G\hookrightarrow FU(G)$
It is the abelianization map $G\to G/[G,G]$
hard.hint: Apply the universal property of the free group to the identity function on $U(G)$.
hard.explain: The counit $\varepsilon_G$ is the evaluation map: $FU(G)$ is the free group on the underlying set of $G$, and $\varepsilon_G$ is the unique homomorphism sending each generator $g\in U(G)$ to $g\in G$ — i.e. 'reduce words in elements of $G$ using $G$'s own multiplication.' This is the universal property of the free group applied to $\mathrm{id}_{U(G)}$.

hard.q: In $\mathsf{Set}$, the coproduct (categorical colimit) of $\{a,b\}$ and $\{0,1,2\}$ has how many elements?
hard.explain: The coproduct in $\mathsf{Set}$ is the disjoint union, so $|A\sqcup B|=|A|+|B|=2+3=5$.

questions.q: A functor $F\colon\mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}}\to\mathsf{Set}$ is called representable if:
choices: $F$ is naturally isomorphic to $\operatorname{Hom}_{\mathcal{C}}(-,X)$ for some $X\in\mathcal{C}$
questions.explain: Representability means $F\cong h_X$ for some object $X$; by Yoneda this $X$ is unique up to unique iso.

questions.q: The tensor product $M\otimes_R N$ of two $R$-modules represents which functor (on $R$-modules $P$)?
questions.explain: By definition, $M\otimes_R N$ represents $P\mapsto\operatorname{Bilin}_R(M\times N, P)$: every $R$-bilinear map $M\times N\to P$ factors uniquely through the canonical $\otimes\colon M\times N\to M\otimes N$.

questions.q: By Yoneda, how many objects (up to isomorphism) satisfy the universal property of the product $A\times B$ in a given category (assuming at least one exists)?
questions.explain: Representing objects are unique up to unique isomorphism — exactly $1$ isomorphism class.

hard.q: Arrange the canonical steps of a proof that products in a category are unique up to unique isomorphism. Let $P=(A\times B,\pi_1,\pi_2)$ and $Q=(A\times B,\rho_1,\rho_2)$ be two candidate products of the same objects $A$ and $B$.
items: Apply the universal property of $Q$ to the cone $(\pi_1,\pi_2)$ on $P$ to obtain a unique map $u\colon P\to Q$ with $\rho_i\circ u=\pi_i$.
Apply the universal property of $P$ to the cone $(\rho_1,\rho_2)$ on $Q$ to obtain a unique map $v\colon Q\to P$ with $\pi_i\circ v=\rho_i$.
Use uniqueness in the universal property of $P$ to conclude $v\circ u=\mathrm{id}_P$ (both sides are the unique map $P\to P$ compatible with $\pi_1,\pi_2$).
Use uniqueness in the universal property of $Q$ to conclude $u\circ v=\mathrm{id}_Q$ (both sides are the unique map $Q\to Q$ compatible with $\rho_1,\rho_2$).
hard.explain: First produce $u$ and $v$ in either order (A, then B). Then verify $vu=\mathrm{id}_P$ via uniqueness inside $P$'s universal property (C), and $uv=\mathrm{id}_Q$ via uniqueness inside $Q$'s (D). The proof is symmetric between $P$ and $Q$, which is the hallmark of a universal-property argument.

hard.q: Which of the following is a counterexample showing that a left adjoint need not reflect limits?
choices: The free group functor $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ does not send a product of sets to the product of free groups
The identity functor on $\mathsf{Set}$ preserves all colimits
hard.hint: Which direction (limits vs colimits) does a LEFT adjoint preserve?
hard.explain: Left adjoints preserve colimits but need not preserve — or reflect — limits. The free group functor $F\colon\mathsf{Set}\to\mathsf{Grp}$ sends a two-element product $X\times Y$ to $F(X\times Y)$, the free group on $X\times Y$, which is generally not the product $F(X)\times F(Y)$ in $\mathsf{Grp}$. This is a concrete illustration that LAPC (left adjoints preserve colimits) has no analogue for limits.

hard.q: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be a category with exactly one object $*$ and $\operatorname{Hom}(*,*)=\{\mathrm{id}\}$. How many distinct functors $F\colon\mathcal{C}\to\mathcal{C}$ are there?
hard.explain: There is only one object, so $F(*)=*$ is forced. The only morphism $\mathrm{id}_*$ must map to a morphism $*\to*$, i.e. to $\mathrm{id}_*$ itself. So the only functor is the identity — exactly $1$.

questions.q: A monoid object in the monoidal category $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$ is:
questions.explain: An abelian group $R$ together with a $\mathbb Z$-bilinear multiplication $R\otimes_{\mathbb Z} R\to R$ (associative) and a unit $\mathbb Z\to R$ is exactly the data of a unital ring.

questions.q: Which coherence diagram must commute in every monoidal category?
choices: Mac Lane's pentagon relating the two associators on a fourfold tensor
questions.explain: Mac Lane's pentagon is one of the two required coherence diagrams (the other is the triangle). The hexagon/naturality of $\sigma$ are extra data for braided/symmetric monoidal categories.

questions.q: How many distinct bracketings of a fourfold tensor product $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D$ are there (Catalan number $C_3$)?
questions.explain: The Catalan number $C_3 = 5$ counts full binary trees with $4$ leaves — $((AB)C)D,\ (A(BC))D,\ (AB)(CD),\ A((BC)D),\ A(B(CD))$. Mac Lane's pentagon says all $5$ become equal under $\alpha$.

hard.q: Mac Lane's coherence theorem for monoidal categories states that:
choices: Every monoidal category is equivalent to a strict monoidal category where associativity and unit isomorphisms are identities
The associator $\alpha$ must be the identity morphism
Pentagon and triangle diagrams hold only when all objects are isomorphic
hard.explain: Mac Lane's coherence theorem says every monoidal category is monoidally equivalent to a strict one (where $\otimes$ is strictly associative and $I\otimes A=A=A\otimes I$ on the nose). This justifies treating parenthesizations as equal 'in practice': any diagram built from associators and unit isomorphisms commutes.

hard.q: A commutative monoid object in a symmetric monoidal category $(\mathcal{C},\otimes,I)$ is a monoid $(M,\mu,\eta)$ satisfying additionally $\mu\circ\sigma_{M,M}=\mu$, where $\sigma$ is the symmetry. In $(\mathsf{Ab},\otimes_{\mathbb Z},\mathbb Z)$, what additional structure does this give over an ordinary ring?
hard.explain: Commutativity of the monoid with respect to the symmetry isomorphism $\sigma$ translates exactly to $ab=ba$ in the ring $M$, i.e. $M$ is a commutative ring.

hard.q: The Catalan number $C_n$ counts the bracketings of an $(n{+}1)$-fold tensor. Using $C_n=\binom{2n}{n}/(n+1)$, compute $C_4$ (the number of bracketings of a $5$-fold tensor $A\otimes B\otimes C\otimes D\otimes E$).
hard.explain: $C_4=\binom{8}{4}/5=70/5=14$. These $14$ bracketings all become equal under iterated application of the associator $\alpha$, by Mac Lane's coherence theorem.
